content.assessment: Viewed through the reform-bundle lens, a procedural presumption-against-closure is a single non-structural safeguard layered onto Hungary's already-centralized governance (F-live-portuguese_reform-01). The lens's core caution applies directly: no isolated procedural fix can substitute for coordinated reform of governance, funding, and quality-levelling mechanisms. The proposal does not address the funding formula, teacher shortage/pay gap, or the church-school selection asymmetry that structurally disadvantages state village schools (F-live-portuguese_reform-04, F-live-portuguese_reform-08), so even if adopted it would operate alongside unresolved equity drivers such as rural disadvantage concentration (F-live-portuguese_reform-03) and legal non-discrimination exposure (F-live-portuguese_reform-09).
opportunities: Could be paired with equity monitoring tied to the segregation case law to strengthen causal accountability for closure decisions (F-live-portuguese_reform-09)
strengths: Adds a documented decision-check within the existing centralized structure without requiring wholesale re-devolution (F-live-portuguese_reform-01)
Could create a record trail relevant to non-discrimination legal scrutiny given ongoing infringement and ECtHR findings (F-live-portuguese_reform-09)
threats: Attribution risk: any apparent stabilization from this measure alone could be wrongly credited to the procedure rather than to co-occurring demographic or funding factors, consistent with the general caution against single-intervention attribution
weaknesses: Is a single procedural lever, not a coordinated bundle addressing funding, teacher supply, or selective-admission asymmetries (F-live-portuguese_reform-04, F-live-portuguese_reform-08)
Does not by itself level quality between church-run and state village schools, which the evidence suggests concentrates disadvantage regardless of closure procedure (F-live-portuguese_reform-08)
